title: Teach environment variables and API keys before Phase 3 APIs
description: 03-real-world-skills/02-working-with-apis.md:95-103 introduces API keys ('a long string of characters that identifies you to the service') and then at 03-real-world-skills/02-working-with-apis.md:137 instructs: 'Use the GitHub personal access token from the GITHUB_TOKEN environment variable.' A complete novice does not know: (1) what an environment variable is, (2) how to set one on their OS (export on bash/zsh, $env on PowerShell, persistent vs session-scoped), (3) how to get a GitHub personal access token (Settings > Developer Settings > Personal access tokens > Generate new token > scopes > copy before leaving the page), (4) the security rule: never paste keys into source files or commit them. The Security chapter (03-real-world-skills/04-security.md:35) warns 'API keys in source code. Tokens in config files that get committed to git.' but the warning comes after the reader has already been told to use GITHUB_TOKEN without being taught how to get or store one safely. Fix: Add a new section early in 03-real-world-skills/02-working-with-apis.md titled 'Environment Variables and Secrets' that teaches: what an env var is, how to set one temporarily (shell command) and persistently (shell profile file or OS env UI), how to create a GitHub PAT with step-by-step clicks, why keys go in env vars and .env files (gitignored) not source, and a tiny walkthrough of reading an env var from Rust (std::env::var).
